## Introduction

Storage management is a critical part of Linux system administration. Traditional disk partitioning can be inflexible when storage needs change.

This is where **Logical Volume Manager** (LVM) becomes powerful. It allows administrators to create flexible storage layers that can grow, shrink, and combine multiple disks.

In this article, I’ll explore LVM through two practical scenarios:

*   Creating and managing LVM using a **virtual disk for safe local practice**
    
*   Using LVM on a real cloud server by attaching a storage volume to an **Amazon EC2** instance backed by **Amazon Elastic Block Store**
    

### Understanding LVM Architecture

LVM introduces an abstraction layer between physical storage and filesystems.

Normal structure:

```plaintext
Disk → Partition → Filesystem
```

LVM structure:

```plaintext
Disk → Physical Volume → Volume Group → Logical Volume → Filesystem
```

## Practicing LVM with a Virtual Disk

Before mounting the volumes

![](https://cdn.hashnode.com/uploads/covers/69abd0460bca1a39768f0d14/e160f239-a496-484f-9807-666e1ea9a817.png align="center")

Before working with real disks, it's safer to simulate one using a file.

### Step 1 — Create a Virtual Disk File

1.  lvm-disk1.img \[size: 1GB\]
    
2.  lvm-disk2.img \[size: 2GB\]
    

```shell
dd if=/dev/zero of=/tmp/lvm-disk1.img bs=1M count=1024
dd if=/dev/zero of=/tmp/lvm-disk2.img bs=2M count=1024
```

*   check the created disks files in the `/tmp` dierectory.
    

![](https://cdn.hashnode.com/uploads/covers/69abd0460bca1a39768f0d14/ee1723df-ebde-4ec3-b06f-9e7536a45aa1.png align="center")

### step 2 — Attach the virtual disk file with a **loop device and check**

```shell
sudo losetup -fP /tmp/lvm-disk1.img
sudo losetup -fP /tmp/lvm-disk2.img
```

`losetup` "plugs in" your virtual disk files so the system sees them as hardware.

*   `-f`: Finds the first **free** loop device automatically.
    
*   `-P`: Scans the file for **partitions** and creates separate nodes if found.
    

**Check the device and Note the device name (e.g., /dev/loop0)**

```shell
losetup -a | grep lvm
```

![](https://cdn.hashnode.com/uploads/covers/69abd0460bca1a39768f0d14/18f9cd47-a094-494d-815a-925dd5f1c686.png align="center")

You should see **loop devices with 1G and 2G sizes**

```shell
lsblk | grep loop48
lsblk | grep loop49
```

![](https://cdn.hashnode.com/uploads/covers/69abd0460bca1a39768f0d14/0969a840-f320-4d5d-82da-e2e37a77492e.png align="center")

### step 3 — **Create Physical Volume**

```shell
sudo pvcreate /dev/loop48
sudo pvcreate /dev/loop49
```

*   If `pvcreate` command not found install it with `sudo apt install lvm2 -y`
    

Verify the created volumes with:

```shell
sudo pvs
```

![](https://cdn.hashnode.com/uploads/covers/69abd0460bca1a39768f0d14/75ed96d1-a93a-41e4-85a4-0bce21720d75.png align="center")

### step 4 — Create Volume Group

We will combine both disks loop48 and loop49 into one storage pool `devops-pv`

and check our disks.

```shell
sudo vgcreate devops-vg /dev/loop48 /dev/loop49

sudo vgs

sudo pvdisplay /dev/loop48

sudo pvdisplay /dev/loop49

sudo vgdisplay
```

![](https://cdn.hashnode.com/uploads/covers/69abd0460bca1a39768f0d14/b5aaabd6-1b56-49dd-b17e-3e526ad838f5.png align="center")

### Step 5— Create a Logical Volume

A **Logical Volume (LV)** is basically a **virtual partition created from the Volume Group**.  
It behaves just like a normal disk partition — we can format it, mount it, and store data in it.

command: tocreate, check

```shell
sudo lvcreate -L 500M -n app-data-lv devops-vg
sudo lvs
sudo lvdisplay
```

*   `lvcreate` → command used to create logical volumes
    
*   `-L 1G` → size of the logical volume (1GB)
    
*   `-n app-data-lv` → name of the logical volume
    
*   `devops-vg` → the volume group we created earlier
    

![](https://cdn.hashnode.com/uploads/covers/69abd0460bca1a39768f0d14/5f1c7c99-0e05-42ac-8402-f624bf9e5d5a.png align="center")

### Step 6 — Create a Filesystem

Right now the logical volume is just raw storage. To actually store files, we need to create a filesystem. We'll use ext4, which is common on many Linux systems.

```shell
sudo mkfs.ext4 /dev/devops-vg/app-data-lv
```

*   formats the logical volume
    
*   prepares it so Linux can store files on it
    

![](https://cdn.hashnode.com/uploads/covers/69abd0460bca1a39768f0d14/5e9ef16f-77cc-4a5e-a3d2-5d220f800add.png align="center")

We can check it with this command: `lsblk -f` , `sudo lvdisplay` and `df -h`

```shell
lsblk -f | tail -6
```

![](https://cdn.hashnode.com/uploads/covers/69abd0460bca1a39768f0d14/30b1a25e-18f8-411c-8af7-1fe72129aefc.png align="center")

### Step 7 — Create a Mount Point and Mount The Logical Volume(app-data-lv)

A mount point is simply a directory where the filesystem will be attached.

```shell
mkdir -p /mnt/app-data
mount /dev/devops-vg/app-data-lv /mnt/app-data
```

This attaches the logical volume to `/app-data`.

Anything stored inside `/app-data` will now be written to the logical volume.

*   To verify we can check with `df -h` , `mout | grep app`
    

![](https://cdn.hashnode.com/uploads/covers/69abd0460bca1a39768f0d14/0d9816df-2989-451f-95d0-600fe313b86c.png align="center")

Verify `df -h /mnt/app-data`

![](data:image/png;base64, align="center")

### Step 8 — Verify Storage Works

Now let's test writing to the mounted storage.

![](https://cdn.hashnode.com/uploads/covers/69abd0460bca1a39768f0d14/eac0d0f4-9d57-4674-9d6a-b46e05975042.png align="center")

## **Extending the Logical Volume**

In this step we extend our existing logical volume by **200MB**.

First, extend the logical volume:

### Extending The Logical Volume

```shell
sudo lvextend -L +200M /dev/devops-vg/app-data-lv
```

![](https://cdn.hashnode.com/uploads/covers/69abd0460bca1a39768f0d14/fca49d8a-12b2-4f6a-b6d1-227668a2fb29.png align="center")

But running `df -h` still we can see it's 500MB not 700MB, that's because after running this command, the **logical volume size increases**, but the filesystem still thinks it is the old size.

So we must resize the filesystem.

### Resizing the file System

```shell
sudo resize2fs /dev/devops-vg/app-data-lv
```

![](https://cdn.hashnode.com/uploads/covers/69abd0460bca1a39768f0d14/aebed8f4-2b48-4cb7-b255-47cd180da0dc.png align="center")

![](https://cdn.hashnode.com/uploads/covers/69abd0460bca1a39768f0d14/b26c80f7-c25c-483c-835b-2d1580306234.png align="center")

## Findings

### Filesystem reserved space

When we created the filesystem using:

```plaintext
mkfs.ext4
```

the ext4 filesystem reserves some space for:

*   filesystem metadata
    
*   journaling
    
*   superblocks
    
*   reserved blocks for the root user
    

This space is **not available for normal users**, so tools like `df` show the **usable filesystem size(637M)**, which can appear smaller.

Ubuntu’s file manager is usually showing the **block device size(724M)**, meaning the total size of the logical volume itself.

## Conclusion

Through this lab, I was able to understand the basic workflow of Linux storage management using LVM (Logical Volume Manager). Instead of working directly with physical disks, LVM allows storage to be organized in layers, which makes disk management much more flexible.

In this exercise:

*   I created virtual disks using the `dd` command.
    
*   Attached those disks as loop devices using `losetup`.
    
*   Initialized them as **Physical Volumes (PV)**.
    
*   Combined the physical volumes into a **Volume Group (VG)**.
    
*   Created a **Logical Volume (LV)** from the volume group.
    
*   Formatted the logical volume using the **ext4 filesystem**.
    
*   Finally mounted it to a directory and verified that it was working.
    

After mounting the logical volume, the system detected it as a separate storage device, similar to adding a new disk. Files created inside the mount directory were stored directly inside the logical volume, which confirmed that the configuration was working correctly.

I also noticed that the usable storage space was slightly smaller than the total size of the virtual disks in MB. This difference happens because some space is used for filesystem metadata and formatting.

Overall, this lab helped me understand how LVM adds a layer of abstraction over physical storage and makes it easier to manage disk space. These concepts are commonly used in Linux servers and cloud environments where storage needs to be flexible and scalable.

In the next stage, I plan to repeat a similar setup on a cloud instance using **EC2 and EBS** so I can connect these Linux storage concepts with real DevOps infrastructure.
